items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to the beach' (talking to someone elsewhere) in Spanish?
items.why: Students confuse ir (to go) and venir (to come). IR = movement AWAY from speaker; VENIR = movement TOWARDS speaker. 'I'm going there' = voy; 'I'm coming here' = vengo. Critical for holidays/travel vocab.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Spain' in Spanish?
items.why: Students drop the 'a' or use a conjugated verb. The near future is IR (conjugated) + A + INFINITIVE. The 'a' is obligatory: voy A comer, vamos A ver, vas A estudiar. Heavily tested for holiday/future plans.

items.q: How do you say 'I travel by train' in Spanish?
items.why: Students use por or con. Transport always uses EN: en coche, en avión, en autobús, en tren, en barco. Exception: a pie (on foot), a caballo (on horseback). Critical holiday/travel vocab.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I am going to travel to France'?
items.why: Correct answer: Voy a viajar a Francia.. “Yo viajaré ya Francia.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: en. “por” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many endings does the regular -ar imperfect tense have?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 endings. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
